coredata.dc:title: Morphology transition of Ag nanoprisms as a platform to design a dual sensor for NADH sensitive assay
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Hallaj, Tooba
Salari, Rana
Amjadi, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, we designed a dual colorimetric and fluorimetric probe based on Ag nanoprisms (AgNPRs) and rhodamine B (Rh B) for NADH analysis. AgNPRs/H2O2 were utilized as a colorimetric reporter (as well as fluorescence quencher) and Rh B was applied as a fluorimetric reporter. The colorimetric probe was developed based on the protecting effect of NADH on AgNPRs/H2O2 which caused a red shift in the surface plasmon resonance (SRP) peak of AgNPRs/H2O2. The observed red shift was proportional to the NADH concentration. On the other hand, the spectral overlap between excitation and emission of Rh B and absorption of AgNPRs/H2O2 in the presence and absence of NADH was different. As a result, the Rh B fluorescence emission, quenched by AgNPRs/H2O2 (due to IFE), was retrieved in the presence of NADH. These facts were employed to establish a dual colorimetric and fluorimetric probe to detect NADH at the range of 15–300 and 30–450 nM, respectively. Both - methods were successfully exploited to NADH assay in human serum samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Anti etching colorimetry
Inner filter effect
Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide
Anisotropic NPs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption spectrum and (b) TEM image of AgNPRs.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum, (b) TEM image of AgNPRs/H2O2 in the absence (right) and presence of NADH (left).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of Rh B (a) alone and (b) in the presence of NADH, (e) in the presence of H2O2 (d) in the presence of AgNPRs/ NADH/ H2O2 and (f) in the presence of AgNPRs/ H2O2 (λex: 555 nm). (B) spectral overlap between excitation (a) and emission (b) spectra of Rh B with UV–Vis absorption spectra of AgNPRs/ H2O2 in the absence (c) presence of NADH (d).

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of AgNPRs/ H2O2 in the presence of NADH different concentrations (15–300 nM), The calibration curve of colorimetric method under optimum conditions is indicated in the inset. (b) Fluorescence spectra of Rh B/AgNPRs/ H2O2 in the presence of different concentration of NADH (30–450 nM) with λex: 555 nm, The calibration curve of fluorescence method under optimum conditions is indicated in the inset.
Tolerance limits of some potentially interfering species in the determination of 100 nM NADH by developed colorimetric probe.

row.entry: Na+, K+, Al3+,, Ca2+, PO4 3-, NH4 +, Sucrose, Glucose, Lactose

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Tolerance limits of some potentially interfering species in the determination of 100 nM NADH by developed fluorimetric probe.

row.entry: Na+, K+, Zn2+, Ca2+, NO3 –, PO4 3-, α-Alanine, NH4 +

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison between the established dual method for determination of NADH with some published methods.
Determination of NADH in serum samples by colorimetric probe.
Determination of NADH in serum samples by fluorimetric probe.

head.ce:title: Morphology transition of Ag nanoprisms as a platform to design a dual sensor for NADH sensitive assay

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cellular and Molecular Research Center, Cellular and Molecular Medicine Institute, Urmia University of Medical Sciences, Urmia 5714783734, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Cellular and Molecular Research Center, Cellular and Molecular Medicine Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Cellular and Molecular Research Center, Cellular and Molecular Medicine Institute, Urmia University of Medical Sciences, Urmia 5714783734, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, we designed a dual colorimetric and fluorimetric probe based on Ag nanoprisms (AgNPRs) and rhodamine B (Rh B) for NADH analysis. AgNPRs/H2O2 were utilized as a colorimetric reporter (as well as fluorescence quencher) and Rh B was applied as a fluorimetric reporter. The colorimetric probe was developed based on the protecting effect of NADH on AgNPRs/H2O2 which caused a red shift in the surface plasmon resonance (SRP) peak of AgNPRs/H2O2. The observed red shift was proportional to the NADH concentration. On the other hand, the spectral overlap between excitation and emission of Rh B and absorption of AgNPRs/H2O2 in the presence and absence of NADH was different. As a result, the Rh B fluorescence emission, quenched by AgNPRs/H2O2 (due to IFE), was retrieved in the presence of NADH. These facts were employed to establish a dual colorimetric and fluorimetric probe to detect NADH at the range of 15–300 and 30–450 nM, respectively. Both - methods were successfully exploited to NADH assay in human serum samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(NAD) and its reduced form NADH, as an important coenzyme for more than 300 dehydrogenases enzymes, play a critical role in a variety of cellular reactions, such as cellular growth, differentiation and especially energy production processes. So, any kind of abnormality in the level of NADH or NAD+ can cause negative effect on cellular activity. On the other hand, amount of NADH in cancer cells which depends on the metabolism of cancer cells, can be affected by some anticancer agents [1,2], so monitoring amount of NADH in the cancer cells can be applied as a screening method in discovery of new anticancer drugs. Based on these facts, detection of NADH is important in a verity of biomedical proposes such as controlling the intracellular metabolism, enzyme assays and drug discovery. Divers methods including electrochemical, chromatographic and spectroscopic methods were reported for monitoring NADH concentration in biological samples [3–6]. However, some methods have been mainly complicate, time consuming and expensive.

Currently dual colorimetric and fluorimetric methods have drawn special attention due to gathering benefits of both methods such as simplicity and cost-effectiveness of colorimetric methods and high sensitivity of fluorimetric methods. Furthermore dual sensors make it possible to do real sample analysis by two different methods which result in more reliable results. Therefore, improved analytical performances including enlarged linear range, enhanced accuracy, and boosted application flexibility can be achieved by dual sensors [7]. Various nanomaterial and organic days have been applied to develop dual colorimetric and fluorimetric sensors to measure different analytes including ions and small molecules [8–11]. We have also established some dual sensors based on colorimetric reporters, such as metal nanoparticles and nanosheets, and fluorophores, such as fluoresceine and doped carbon quantum dots, for detection of mancozeb [12], uric acid [13], 6-mercaptopurine [14] and isoniazid [15].

Although there has been no report about the dual colorimetric and fluorimetric method for NADH assay, some individual colorimetric and fluorimetric methods have been already reported for this purpose. For example, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) were applied to develop florescence -based sensors for detection of NADH and dehydrogenase enzyme assays [16]. However, these methods are not suitable for biological analysis due to heavy metal toxicity of QDs as well as their laborious synthesis and modification process. As well, several colorimetric methods have been developed based on in-situ formation of AuNPs or core–shell NPs by the NADH as reducing agent [17–19]. Au nanorods have been also applied to develop an anti-etching colorimetric method for analysis of NADH [20]. Despite simple and cost-effective preparation methods and more stability of Ag nanoprisms (AgNPRs) compared to Au nanorods, there is no report about the AgNPR based colorimetric sensor for detection of NADH.

Concerning the importance of NADH assay in the biological samples and superior properties of AgNPRs, we exploited AgNPRs as a platform to develop a simple, sensitive and cost- effective dual sensor for NADH analysis. AgNPRs/H2O2 (as a colorimetric reporter and fluorescence quencher) and a fluorescent organic dye, rhodamine B (Rh B), were utilized to develop a shape transformation-based colorimetric and turn-on fluorimetric sensor to detect NADH in biological samples.

All chemicals were of analytical grade. Doubly distillated water was utilized during experiments. NADH, AgNO3 and PVP (polyvinyl propylene, K10) were bought from Sigma. Rh B, H2O2 (30 wt%), C6H5Na3O7·2H2O and NaBH4 were acquired from Merck. Human serum samples were obtained from Tabriz blood transfusion organization.

The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorbance and fluorescence measurements were done by absorption spectrophotometer (Cary 100, Varian, Australia) and photoluminescence spectrofluorimeter (FP-8300, Jasko, Japan), respectively. The morphology and size distribution of AgNPRs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM; Leo 906, Zeiss, Germany).

Various amounts of NADH standard solution or real samples were added into a test tube containing 500 µL of AgNPRs, 50 µL of Britton-Robinson buffer (BRB) (0.04 M) and then incubated for 5 min. After that 10 µL of H2O2 (0.01 M) was added to the tube and the final volume was adjusted to 1.0 mL by distillated water. After 10 min incubation at the ambient temperature, the mixture absorption spectrum was measured in a 1.0 cm quartz cuvette on UV–vis spectrophotometer.

For fluorescence measurements, 200 µL of Rh B (1 µM) was added to above mentioned mixture and the last volume was reached to 2.0 mL by distillated water. At last, the solution was transferred into a 1.0 cm quartz cuvette and the fluorescence intensity was measured at 575 nm emission wavelength (λex: 555 nm, slit width: 5 nm).

1.8 mL of ZnSO4 (0.1 M) and 2.0 mL of Ba(OH)2 (0.1 M) were poured into a sample tube including 500 µL of human serum sample and followed by centrifuging (15 min, 6000 rpm) to precipitate serum proteins. The obtained plain supernatant was transferred to a volumetric flask, 5.0 mL, and diluted to the volume by distillated water. An appropriate amount of obtained solution was applied to NADH assay as mentioned in the general process.

We planned to design a simple and sensitive dual colorimetric and fluorimetric probe based on shape conversion of AgNPRs and their inner filter effect (IFE) on an organic fluorescent dye Rh B for detection of NADH in biological samples. For this purpose, AgNPRs synthesized according to our previous work from AgNO3, H2O2, C6H5Na3O7·2H2O, PVP and NaBH4 [21]. Then, as-prepared NPs were characterized by TEM images and UV–vis absorption spectrum. Appearance of AgNPRs’ characteristic absorption bands at 335, 500 and 770 nm in the UV–vis spectrum indicated the successful formation of AgNPRs (Fig. 1 a). The TEM image of AgNPRs confirmed their triangular prismatic shape with edge length around 40 nm (Fig. 1b).

As-prepared AgNPRs and H2O2, as an etching agent, were applied to design the NADH colorimetric probe. Fig. 2 a indicates the effect of H2O2 on the AgNPRs’ solution color and SPR peak in the presence and absence of NADH. The observed color change in experimental solution and blue shift in AgNPRs’ SPR peak in the presence of H2O2 can be assigned to the transformation of AgNPRs to Ag nanodisks. The reason for SPR shift as a result of shape transformation is that the frequency of the coherent oscillation of conduction electrons at the surface of nanoparticles is not only dependent on the density and effective mass of the electrons, but also on the size and shape of the charge distribution [22]. H2O2 is known as a powerful oxidant with higher E0 = 0.867 V in the alkaline condition compared to Ag with E0 = 0.799 V, so it can react with Ag atoms on the corner sites of AgNPRs and degenerate them to Ag+ ions [23–26]. When NADH was added to the solution, the extent of color change and SPR peak shift became smaller. At sufficiently high concentration of NADH, the SPR peak remained at the original position (around 770 nm). We suspected that NADH not only can react with H2O2 as a reducing agent [3,20] but also can adsorb on the surface of AgNPRs via forming NH-Ag bond [27] and protect AgNPRs from etching by H2O2 (The experimental evidence was reported in Electronic Supplementary Information ESI, Fig. S1). TEM imaging was applied to confirm these assertions. The results confirm the conversion of triangle AgNPRs to nanodisk with the diameter around 25 nm in AgNPRs-H2O2 solution as well as the protection of AgNPRs from etching in AgNPRs-NADH-H2O2 solution (Fig. 2b).

At the next step, considering the maximum overlap between excitation and emission spectra of Rh B (λex = 555 nm, λem = 575 nm) and SPR peak of AgNPRs/H2O2 (around 580 nm), it was applied as the best fluorophore to design the fluorimetric probe among various fluorophores such as carbon quantum dots (λex = 400 nm, λem = 525 nm), fluorescein (λex = 495 nm, λem = 515 nm) and Au nanoclusters (λex = 400 nm, λem = 600 nm). For this purpose, the effect of AgNPRs/H2O2 on the Rh B fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of NADH was studied. As can be seen in Fig. 3 A, the fluorescence emission of Rh B quenched with adding AgNPRs/H2O2 mixture while it was restored with adding AgNPRs/NADH/H2O2 mixture. It can be attributed to the IFE of AgNPRs/H2O2 on Rh B. The spectral overlap of Rh B emission and excitation spectra and the AgNPRs/H2O2 absorption spectrum in the presence and absence of NADH is indicated in Fig. 3B. Since the spectral overlap in the absence of NADH is much higher than that in its presence, the Rh B/AgNPRs/H2O2 fluorescence intensity retrieved with adding NADH.

These phenomena were exploited to design the dual colorimetric and fluorimetric method for determination of NADH. The AgNPRs’ SPR wavelength shift and Rh B retrieved fluorescence intensity were measured as analytical signals in colorimetric and fluorimetric methods, respectively.

To achieve the maximum sensitivity for detection of NADH, the influence of some experimental parameters, including pH, reaction time and amount of AgNPRs were studied on the wavelength shift of AgNPRs/ H2O2 in the presence of NADH. Subsequently, the figure of merits of designed methods were obtained under the optimized condition (Condition: Ag NPRs (500 µL), H2O2 (0.1 mM), pH = 8, BRB (2.0 mM), incubation time 10 min, related curves were reported in ESI). As can be seen in Fig. 4 a, the SPR peak wavelength variations as well as the color changes of AgNPRs were proportional to the concentration of NADH at the range of 15–300 nM. So, it is a sensitive colorimetric method for NADH with the detection limit and relative standard deviation (RSD%, 200 nM) of 5.0 nM and 2.0%, respectively. Furthermore, the retrieved fluorescence signal of Rh B/AgNPRs/ H2O2 in the presence of NADH linearly increased with increasing NADH concentration in the range of 30–450 nM. The calibration curve was indicated in Fig. 4b. The detection limit and relative standard deviation (RSD%, 200 nM) of fluorimetric method were 12 nM and 2.1%, respectively.

The selectivity of developed methods was evaluated by investigation of the effect of some organic and inorganic components which can be found in biological fluids. The tolerable concentration ratios for interferences was calculated in relative error of < 5%. The reported results in Tables 1a,b
demonstrate the desirable selectivity of dual method for NADH assay.

Table 2 indicates the comparison between the analytical performance of the dual probe and some previous colorimetric and fluorimetric probes. As can be seen, the developed method has lower detection limit and is more sensitive than others. Specially, the former fluorimetric probes detected micromolar level of NADH while its nanomolar amounts can be detected by our fluorimetric probe. Furthermore, in this study the non-aggregation colorimetric method was applied for NADH assay, which is generally more selective and anti-interference for analysis of complex biological samples compared to the aggregation based colorimetric methods [28]. In addition, the established colorimetric and fluorimetric probes are simple and cost effective compared to some reported methods which suffer from complicate and expensive processes.

The potential of developed methods was examined in the analysis of NADH in human serum samples. The obtained recoveries were confirming the applicability of established methods in the analysis of real sample. Moreover, the Student’s t-test values calculated for all measurements indicated the accuracy of methods. The results were reported in Tables 3a,b
. Indeed, we analyzed real samples by both colorimetric and fluorimetric methods which resulted in more reliable results.

In summary, a sensitive dual colorimetric and fluorimetric method was established to determine NADH by using AgNPRs/H2O2 (as a colorimetric reporter and quencher) and Rh B (as a fluorimetric reporter). The dual sensor possesses the following advantages: (1) the simple synthesis method of AgNPRs and commercial availability of applied reagents (including H2O2 and Rh B) made the assay convenient, rapid, and cost-effective; (2) the dual sensor was able to detect NADH in nanomolar levels with desirable selectivity; (3) the assay could display colorimetric and fluorescent dual signal, making the detection results more reliable. Furthermore, the applicability of developed sensor for detection of NADH in human serum samples was indicated with satisfactory results. We believe that the developed dual sensor can be also exploited for assaying of dehydrogenase enzymes’ activity and screening of their inhibitors via monitoring of NADH concentration in the enzymatic reaction.

Tooba Hallaj: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition. Rana Salari: Investigation, Formal analysis, Validation. Mohammad Amjadi: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank Urmia University of Medical Sciences for the support provided (The project number: 2724). This project approved by the Research Ethic Committee at the Urmia University of Medical Sciences, Ethical code: IR. UMSU.REC.1399.043).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2022.114043.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
